title: Development
text:
The docs/ directory is a Quarto website project. Pages are authored as .qmd files and rendered to static HTML in docs/_site/.


quarto render docs/
The output is written to docs/_site/. Open docs/_site/index.html in a browser to preview. No pipeline output or R packages are required to render.




Create a .qmd file in docs/ with YAML front matter matching the existing pattern:
---
title: "Page Title"
subtitle: "Short description"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---
Add a navbar entry in docs/_quarto.yml:
- text: "Page Title"
  href: page-title.qmd
Add a bullet to the Reports list in docs/index.qmd.
Render and verify:
quarto render docs/




Configuration lives in docs/_quarto.yml:



Setting
Value
Purpose




project.type
website
Multi-page site with shared navbar


project.output-dir
_site
Render target (git-ignored)

nemascan-sims-nf
Navbar brand text


format.html.theme
cosmo
Bootstrap theme


format.html.toc
true
Per-page table of contents


format.html.toc-depth

TOC heading depth



Pages that set embed-resources: true produce self-contained HTML files with all CSS/JS inlined, which is useful for sharing individual pages outside the site context.



The rendered site and Quarto working files are git-ignored:

docs/_site/ — rendered HTML output (in .gitignore)
docs/.quarto/ — Quarto project cache (in docs/.gitignore)

title: Development
text:
Publishing to GitHub Pages
The documentation site can be published as a GitHub Pages site for the repository. There are two approaches depending on whether you want to commit the rendered HTML or build it in CI.

Publish from a Branch (No CI Required)
This approach uses the quarto publish command to push rendered HTML to a dedicated gh-pages branch. No CI configuration is needed.
One-time setup:

Run quarto publish from the project root, targeting GitHub Pages:
quarto publish gh-pages docs/
This renders the site, creates or updates a gh-pages branch containing only the rendered output, and pushes it to the remote.
In the GitHub repository settings, go to Settings > Pages and configure:

Source: Deploy from a branch
Branch: gh-pages, folder / (root)


The site will be available at https://andersenlab.github.io/nemascan-sims-nf/.
Branch note: quarto publish gh-pages works from any branch. It renders the .qmd files in your current working tree and pushes the output to the gh-pages branch, regardless of which branch you are on. This means you can preview-publish docs from a feature branch (e.g. doc-updates) before merging to main.
To update the published site after changing .qmd files:
quarto publish gh-pages docs/
This is a manual step — the site is not automatically rebuilt on push.

title: Development
text:
Running Tests

Test Tiers
The test suite has three tiers with different requirements:









Tier
Files
Requirements
Skips automatically when




Unit
all test-*.R except integration/cross-val
Static fixtures only; no pipeline run needed
—


Integration
test-db_structure.R, test-cross_validation.R
Pipeline output (TEST_DB_DIR, TEST_WORK_DIR)
Env vars unset


Cross-validation
test-assessment_cross_validation.R
Both DB and legacy output
TEST_LEGACY_ASSESSMENT unset



Run all tiers with a single command; integration and cross-validation tests skip automatically when their environment variables are absent:
Rscript tests/run_tests.R


Unit Tests
Unit tests use static fixtures and do not require pipeline output. Tests are organized by functional area in tests/testthat/:







File
What it tests




test-library_check.R
R library file existence and function availability after sourcing setup.R


test-read_raw_gwa.R
read_raw_gwa_file() for .fastGWA and .mlma formats; column normalization; LOCO deduplication


test-read_bim.R
read_bim_file() parses .bim format; column names; CHROM:POS marker construction


test-genotype_storage.R
Round-trip write_genotype_matrix() / read_genotype_matrix(); schema validation (CHROM, POS, strain, allele); existence checks; verifies genotype files are excluded from the markers DuckDB view


test-trait_storage.R
generate_trait_id() determinism (same params → same ID), uniqueness (different params → different IDs), and golden-value hash; round-trips for write_trait_metadata(), write_causal_variants(), write_phenotype_data(); overwrite = TRUE retry behavior; phenotype_exists() existence check


test-significance.R
BF and EIGEN significance thresholds; verifies significance calls are identical whether log10p is computed on-the-fly or stored


test-write_gwa_to_db.R
GWA data writes to Parquet DB with correct schema and values; partition path correctness


test-hash_generation.R
generate_marker_set_id(), generate_trait_id(), generate_mapping_id() golden-value hashes; SHA-256 stability across calls


test-safe_log10p.R
safe_log10p() clamping at LOG10P_MAX; vector handling; known ground-truth values


test-module_sourcing.R
All db_migration module R scripts reference R_SOURCE_DIR and are readable


test-assessment.R
R/assessment.R functions: load_causal_variants(), QTL detection, designate_qtl() categories


test-schema_changes.R
mappings_schema() field count (8), required FK columns, exclusion of N and log10p




Test Fixtures
tests/fixtures/ contains static synthetic inputs for unit tests:







File
Description




test_genotype_matrix.tsv
5 markers × 3 strains, wide-format TSV with -1/1/NA encoding — includes a heterozygous (NA) case to verify encoding is preserved


test_sims.phen
3-strain phenotype file in GCTA .phen format (FID IID value, space-delimited, no header)


test_sims.par
GCTA causal variant parameter file (.par format) with 4 causal variants; used by test-assessment.R


test.bim
PLINK .bim marker file with 50 markers; used by test-read_bim.R


test.mlma
Raw GCTA .mlma output (LOCO mode); used by test-read_raw_gwa.R


test.fastGWA
Raw GCTA .fastGWA output (inbred mode); used by test-read_raw_gwa.R and test-write_gwa_to_db.R


test_loco_dedup.mlma
Deduplicated LOCO .mlma for duplicate-marker handling tests


test_eigen.txt
Eigenvalue count for significance threshold computation; used by test-significance.R


test_mapping.tsv
Processed mapping with precomputed log10p column; used by test-significance.R and test-safe_log10p.R






Integration Tests
Integration tests validate that the full pipeline produces correct output end-to-end. Unit tests check individual R functions with synthetic fixtures; integration tests use actual pipeline output to catch bugs that only appear with real GCTA output — edge cases in file format parsing, Hive partition path construction, DuckDB view registration, and Nextflow channel wiring.

test-db_structure.R — verifies the Parquet database written by the DB_MIGRATION_* modules has the expected directory structure, metadata files, and schema-conformant data queryable via DuckDB.
test-cross_validation.R — compares what was written to the Parquet DB against the raw .fastGWA/.mlma files in the Nextflow work directory to verify round-trip fidelity (no dropped rows, truncated values, or mangled column types).

Integration tests require pipeline output collected by tests/collect_test_data.sh:
# Run pipeline (requires Docker) and collect outputs
bash tests/collect_test_data.sh

# Collect from a previous run (no pipeline re-run)
bash tests/collect_test_data.sh --collect-only

# Remove previous results before running
bash tests/collect_test_data.sh --clean

# Run integration tests with collected data
TEST_DB_DIR=tests/integration_data/db \
TEST_WORK_DIR=tests/.nf-work \
TEST_LEGACY_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
Rscript tests/run_tests.R
The default pipeline run inside collect_test_data.sh uses -profile test,docker, so Docker must be available for a local integration test collection run. Integration test files skip automatically when the required environment variables are not set.


Cross-Validation Tests
Two test files compare the DB assessment path against the legacy R path:

test-assessment_cross_validation.R — compares db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv (DB path) against simulation_assessment_results.tsv (legacy path). Computes three concordance scores per replicate: detection concordance, designation concordance, and interval overlap (Jaccard). Requires both TEST_LEGACY_ASSESSMENT and TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT.
test-cross_validation.R — compares DB Parquet contents against raw GWA files in the Nextflow work directory to verify round-trip accuracy. Requires TEST_DB_DIR and TEST_WORK_DIR.

Both tests skip automatically unless all required env vars are set. Both require --legacy_assess to have been passed when collecting test data (so that the legacy output files exist alongside the DB output).

title: Development
text:
R Package Lockfile
renv.lock at the project root records the exact versions of all R packages installed in the andersenlab/r_packages:20250519 container. This file is a reproducibility audit artifact — it lets you verify which package versions produced a given set of simulation results and reproduce the environment locally if needed.

What is covered
The lockfile covers packages used by R_.* Nextflow processes (bin/ scripts):







Script
Key packages




bin/Get_GenoMatrix_Eigen.R
Rcpp, dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, stringr, readr, glue, purrr, coop, RSpectra


bin/Create_Causal_QTLs.R
dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, stringr, readr, IRanges, fuzzyjoin, glue, purrr


bin/Get_GCTA_Intervals.R
dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2, stringr, readr, glue, purrr, ggbeeswarm


bin/Assess_Sims.R
dplyr, tidyr, data.table, GenomicRanges, purrr, glue



DB_MIGRATION_.* processes run in a separate container (andersenlab/migrate-sims:20260202) and are not covered by this lockfile.


Reproducing the environment locally
# Install renv if not present
install.packages("renv")

# Restore the exact package versions recorded in renv.lock
renv::restore(lockfile = "renv.lock")


Regenerating the lockfile
Regenerate renv.lock whenever the r_packages container is rebuilt:
# Local (Docker)
bash scripts/generate_renv_lock.sh docker

# HPC / Rockfish (Singularity)
bash scripts/generate_renv_lock.sh singularity
The script requires an explicit runtime argument (docker or singularity). It pulls andersenlab/r_packages:20250519, runs renv::snapshot(type = "all") inside the container via a bind-mounted project root, and writes renv.lock. Commit the updated file.

nextflow run main.nf -profile test -stub-run

# Variable architecture
nextflow run main.nf -profile test_variable -stub-run

title: Development
text:
Thread-count determinism
All GCTA steps except --mlma-loco are pinned to --thread-num 1 to ensure reproducible floating-point results across local and HPC runs. Multi-threaded BLAS uses non-deterministic partial-sum reduction order — differences propagate from GRM construction through REML, PCA, and the LMM solve into BETA, SE, and P-values.








GCTA step
--thread-num
Rationale




--make-grm-inbred / --make-grm
1
Errors here propagate to all downstream steps


--reml
1
Governs Vp upscaling decision; must be consistent at borderline values


--simu-qt
1
Ensures phenotype simulation is deterministic for a given random seed


--make-bK-sparse
1
Eliminates residual non-determinism in sparse encoding


--pca 1
1
Eigenvector iteration sensitive to BLAS reduction order


--fastGWA-mlm-exact
1
LMM solve; diverges between local and HPC without pinning


--mlma-loco
configurable (task.cpus)
Per-chromosome refits are independent; highest compute cost; least numerically sensitive step



The gcta_make_grm and gcta_simulate_phenotypes Rockfish labels are configured with cpus = 1 since all their GCTA operations are single-threaded. The gcta_perform_gwa label retains cpus = 4 to provide CPU headroom for --mlma-loco on larger datasets.

BLAS implementation in the production container
gcta64 is a static executable — ldd returns “not a dynamic executable”. Inspect the binary with strings to identify the embedded BLAS:
docker run --rm --platform linux/amd64 \
  quay.io/biocontainers/gcta:1.94.1--h9ee0642_0 \
  sh -c 'strings $(which gcta64) | grep -i -E "mkl|openblas|goto|omp_set_num_threads"'
Result: Intel oneMKL, statically linked, with GNU OpenMP as the thread runtime (gomp_ialias_omp_set_num_threads).

How MKL + OpenMP cause non-determinism in GCTA
Intel MKL parallelises dense linear algebra by splitting work across OpenMP threads and reducing partial results at the end. Floating-point addition is not associative — (a + b) + c ≠ a + (b + c) in IEEE 754 — so the order in which thread-local partial sums are accumulated determines the final value. OpenMP does not guarantee a fixed reduction order, and MKL actively varies its internal tiling and accumulation strategy based on the thread count and the SIMD instruction set available at runtime. The consequences for each GCTA operation are:







Operation
How threading introduces non-determinism




--make-grm[-inbred]
Each GRM entry is a sum of allele products over all markers. MKL threads split the marker axis; partial sums are reduced in thread-completion order, which varies run to run.


--reml
REML is an iterative EM algorithm driven by matrix-vector products on the GRM. Non-deterministic accumulation in each MV product shifts the convergence path, producing different Vp estimates and affecting the check_vp.py upscaling decision at borderline values.


--pca 1
Power-iteration eigenvector computation applies repeated MV products. Accumulated rounding differences rotate the eigenvector slightly each run, changing the PC1 covariate value passed to the LMM.


--fastGWA-mlm-exact
The exact LMM solve applies a sparse Cholesky factorization. Thread-parallel factorization produces different fill-in and accumulated roundoff, shifting BETA, SE, and P-values for markers near the significance boundary.


--mlma-loco
Same Cholesky-based LMM, applied once per chromosome. Individual chromosome solves are independent, so parallelism across chromosomes is safe — only within-solve threading (controlled by --thread-num) introduces non-determinism.



MKL also exposes MKL_DYNAMIC, which allows MKL to adjust its own thread count at runtime independent of the application. With MKL_DYNAMIC=TRUE (the MKL default), MKL may use fewer threads than requested if it judges the workload too small — meaning thread count, and therefore reduction order, can differ between runs on the same machine. Pinning --thread-num 1 disables intra-operation parallelism entirely, making reduction order deterministic regardless of MKL_DYNAMIC.


Thread control hierarchy
--thread-num 1 is the primary control and is sufficient for determinism. OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 and MKL_NUM_THREADS=1 are independent env-var overrides that remain effective if any code path bypasses the GCTA flag (e.g., a direct MKL call from a dependency). These are noted as a future belt-and-suspenders hardening option.

title: Development
text:
HPC Execution (Rockfish)
The rockfish profile targets the Rockfish cluster at Johns Hopkins, using SLURM for job scheduling and Singularity for containers.

Environment Setup
export NXF_VER=24.10.4
export NXF_SINGULARITY_CACHEDIR=/vast/eande106/singularity


Prerequisites

Nextflow >=24.10.0,<25.0.0 (pinned via NXF_VER)
SLURM account eande106; partition parallel (default) and bigmem (legacy QTL detection only)
Singularity images cached at /vast/eande106/singularity (configured via singularity.cacheDir in conf/rockfish.config)



Running the Pipeline on Rockfish
NXF_VER=24.10.4 nextflow run main.nf \
  -profile rockfish \
  --strainfile strains.tsv
  # vcf is specified per-group in the strainfile (see README: Strainfile Format)
The rockfish profile sets executor = 'slurm', enables Singularity with autoMounts = true, and applies per-process CPU/RAM/time/retry settings. Key process resources from conf/rockfish.config:

Collecting Integration Test Data on Rockfish
Run the pipeline on the cluster, then use --collect-only on the login node to gather outputs without re-running:
# After the pipeline completes on SLURM:
bash tests/collect_test_data.sh --collect-only
Copy tests/integration_data/ to your local machine, then run the integration tests locally:
TEST_DB_DIR=tests/integration_data/db \
TEST_WORK_DIR=tests/.nf-work \
TEST_LEGACY_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
Rscript tests/run_tests.R
Note: TEST_WORK_DIR points to the Nextflow work directory. If collecting from a remote cluster, test-cross_validation.R (which reads raw GWA files from the work directory) must be run on the cluster or the work directory must also be copied locally.


Stub Runs on Rockfish
Stub runs bypass SLURM submission and verify process wiring without executing real computations:
NXF_VER=24.10.4 nextflow run main.nf \
  -profile rockfish \
  --strainfile strains.tsv \
  -stub-run
  # vcf is specified per-group in the strainfile (see README: Strainfile Format)

title: Development
text:
Container Updates
All pipeline processes run inside versioned, date-tagged Docker/Singularity images. Container references live in two files:



File
Used by




conf/docker.config
Local runs (-profile docker)


conf/rockfish.config
HPC runs (-profile rockfish)



When a container image is updated, both config files must be changed in the same PR. The PYTHON_.* container also has a companion requirements.txt that must be kept in sync.

Updating the Python container (andersenlab/numpy)
bin/create_causal_vars.py is the only pipeline script with third-party Python dependencies (numpy, pandas). These are pinned in requirements.txt at the repository root. When the andersenlab/numpy image is updated:

Update the container tag in conf/docker.config and conf/rockfish.config:
withName: 'PYTHON_.*' {
    container = "andersenlab/numpy:YYYYMMDD"
}
Extract the exact library versions from the new image:
docker run --rm andersenlab/numpy:YYYYMMDD \
  python -c "import numpy, pandas; print('numpy:', numpy.__version__); print('pandas:', pandas.__version__)"
Update requirements.txt with the extracted versions:
# Python dependencies for bin/create_causal_vars.py
# Pinned to versions present in andersenlab/numpy:YYYYMMDD
numpy==X.Y.Z
pandas==X.Y.Z
Verify the pinned versions match the container:
grep -E '^(numpy|pandas)==[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$' requirements.txt | wc -l
# must print: 2

The requirements.txt is a documentation/local-dev reference — no pip install step is embedded in the Nextflow scripts. The container is the sealed execution environment; requirements.txt records what it contains.


Updating other containers
For all other containers (bcftools, plink, GCTA, R, DB migration), update the tag in conf/docker.config and conf/rockfish.config and verify with a stub run:
nextflow run main.nf -profile test,docker -stub-run

title: Development
text:
Generating Test Data
Test VCF files are not committed to the repository due to their size. Four generate scripts produce subsetted VCFs from the corresponding CaeNDR isotype release. All scripts accept either a local file path or a direct HTTPS URL from the CaeNDR S3 bucket — using a URL is recommended because bcftools fetches only chromosomes I, II, V via tabix random access, avoiding a multi-GB download.
Prerequisites: bcftools >= 1.16, tabix (htslib).

C. elegans test VCF (test / test_variable profiles)
bash data/test/generate_test_vcf.sh /path/to/WI.20220216.hard-filter.isotype.vcf.gz
Produces data/test/test.vcf.gz (chromosomes I/II/V) and its tabix index. The script reads the strains column from the 6-column test_strains.txt strainfile. This VCF is used by the test and test_variable Nextflow profiles.

Note (awk fix): This script was updated alongside the S03 strainfile format change — the strains column is now extracted with awk -F'\t' 'NR>1 {print $6}' (column 6, tab-delimited) instead of the old awk '{print $2}'.



Multi-species test VCFs (test_three_species profile)
The test_three_species profile exercises the full mixed-species code path. It requires VCF files for all three supported species (C. elegans, C. briggsae, C. tropicalis).

Strainfile structure
data/test/test_strains_three_species.txt has six groups — two per species — covering both a small-strain and a large-strain population for each:



Group

The single-population groups ({sp}.test) use all 200 strains. The multi-population groups split those 200 strains evenly into popA (first 100) and popB (last 100). All groups for a species share the same VCF, so only one VCF per species is needed.


Generating the CE, CB, and CT test VCFs
CaeNDR release pages for finding the latest HTTPS URLs:

C. elegans: https://caendr.org/data/data-release/c-elegans/latest
C. briggsae: https://caendr.org/data/data-release/c-briggsae/latest
C. tropicalis: https://caendr.org/data/data-release/c-tropicalis/latest

Run the generate scripts from the project root, passing the S3 URL from each release page:
bash data/test/generate_test_ce_vcf.sh \
  "https://caendr-open-access-data-bucket.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/dataset_release/c_elegans/YYYYMMDD/variation/WI.YYYYMMDD.hard-filter.isotype.vcf.gz"

bash data/test/generate_test_cb_vcf.sh \
  "https://caendr-open-access-data-bucket.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/dataset_release/c_briggsae/YYYYMMDD/variation/WI.YYYYMMDD.hard-filter.isotype.vcf.gz"

bash data/test/generate_test_ct_vcf.sh \
  "https://caendr-open-access-data-bucket.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/dataset_release/c_tropicalis/YYYYMMDD/variation/WI.YYYYMMDD.hard-filter.isotype.vcf.gz"
Each script:

Extracts the 8-digit release date from the source VCF filename
Subsets to chromosomes I, II, V and the first 200 strains by default (pass a custom strain-list file as a second argument to change the selection)
Creates dated symlinks: test_ce.YYYYMMDD.vcf.gz → test_ce.vcf.gz (and .tbi)
Prints the assembled small (100-strain) and large (200-strain) strainfile rows

After running all three scripts, commit the six dated symlinks and the strainfile:
git add data/test/test_ce.YYYYMMDD.vcf.gz data/test/test_ce.YYYYMMDD.vcf.gz.tbi
git add data/test/test_cb.YYYYMMDD.vcf.gz data/test/test_cb.YYYYMMDD.vcf.gz.tbi
git add data/test/test_ct.YYYYMMDD.vcf.gz data/test/test_ct.YYYYMMDD.vcf.gz.tbi
git add data/test/test_strains_three_species.txt



Running the three-species test
Once VCF files are generated and the strainfile is populated:
nextflow run . -profile test_three_species,docker
A stub run (no VCF files required) verifies channel wiring and strainfile parsing for all six groups:
nextflow run . -profile test_three_species -stub-run

title: Development
text:
Project Structure
nemascan-sims-nf/
 ├── main.nf                     Pipeline entrypoint
 ├── nextflow.config             Parameters and profiles
 ├── conf/                       Execution profiles (rockfish, docker)
 ├── modules/                    Nextflow process definitions
 │   ├── bcftools/               VCF manipulation processes
 │   ├── plink/                  LD pruning, binary fileset creation
 │   ├── gcta/                   GRM construction, GWAS mapping, simulation
 │   ├── python/                 Causal variant selection, variance check
 │   ├── r/                      Legacy QTL detection and assessment
 │   ├── db_migration/           DB-path ingestion, analysis, assessment
 │   │   ├── write_marker_set/
 │   │   ├── write_genotype_matrix/   ◄── Phase 5: long-format genotype storage
 │   │   ├── write_gwa_to_db/
 │   │   ├── write_trait_data/        ◄── Phase 5: trait metadata/causal/phenotype
 │   │   ├── aggregate_metadata/
 │   │   ├── analyze_qtl/
 │   │   └── assess_sims/
 │   └── local/                  Helper processes (contig info, eigens)
 ├── bin/                        Pipeline scripts (available to all processes)
 ├── R/                          Shared R library (sourced by db_migration + tests)
 ├── data/test/                  Test VCF, parameter files, strain lists
 ├── tests/
 │   ├── testthat/               R/testthat unit test files
 │   │   ├── test-genotype_storage.R  ◄── Phase 5: genotype matrix round-trip
 │   │   └── test-trait_storage.R     ◄── Phase 5: trait ID, metadata, causal, phenotype
 │   ├── fixtures/               Static inputs for unit tests
 │   │   ├── test_genotype_matrix.tsv ◄── Phase 5: 5 markers × 3 strains
 │   │   └── test_sims.phen           ◄── Phase 5: 3-strain phenotype fixture
 │   └── notebooks/              Cross-validation analysis notebooks
 └── docs/                       Quarto documentation site
Each module directory contains a main.nf with the process definition and optionally a resources/ directory with scripts added to $PATH via nextflow.enable.moduleBinaries = true.

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
The nemascan-sims-nf pipeline can produce QTL assessment results through two independent paths:

DB-path (default) — stores GWA results in a Parquet/DuckDB database and performs QTL analysis by querying that database.
Legacy path (opt-in via --legacy_assess) — uses R scripts operating on intermediate files produced during the pipeline run.

Both paths analyze the same GWA output from GCTA_PERFORM_GWA and should produce identical QTL detection, interval boundaries, and significance classifications. The cross-validation analysis quantifies their agreement using three per-replicate concordance scores.

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
The nemascan-sims-nf pipeline can produce QTL assessment results through two independent paths:

Legacy path (opt-in via --legacy_assess) — uses R scripts operating on intermediate files produced during the pipeline run.

Both paths analyze the same GWA output from GCTA_PERFORM_GWA and should produce identical QTL detection, interval boundaries, and significance classifications. The cross-validation analysis quantifies their agreement using three per-replicate concordance scores.

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
When to Use This
Run the cross-validation when:

Validating changes to the DB-path analysis code against the legacy path as a reference
Verifying that a new pipeline deployment produces consistent results
Comparing assessment outputs from different pipeline versions or configurations

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
Concordance Scores
For each mapping entry — a unique combination of simulation replicate (nQTL, simREP, h2, maf, strain_set_id), mapping method, and significance threshold — three scores are computed on a 0-to-1 scale:









Score
What it measures
1 means
0 means




Detection
Detected/Simulated flag agreement per QTL row
All QTL rows agree
None agree


Designation
designate_qtl() outcome category agreement
All designations match
None match


Interval Overlap
Jaccard overlap of [startPOS, endPOS] intervals
Boundaries match perfectly
No overlap

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
Running the Cross-Validation

1. Run the pipeline with both paths
NXF_VER=24.10.4 nextflow run main.nf -profile test,docker --legacy_assess
This produces two assessment TSVs in the output directory:

simulation_assessment_results.tsv (legacy path)
db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv (DB path)



2. Run the automated test suite
The test file tests/testthat/test-assessment_cross_validation.R asserts that all three concordance scores are 1.0 for every mapping entry. It also verifies that generate_mapping_id() is deterministic: the same simulation parameters always produce the same 20-character hex mapping_id across repeated invocations. It runs as part of the standard test suite:
TEST_LEGACY_ASSESSMENT=/path/to/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=/path/to/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
Rscript tests/run_tests.R
If using the tests/collect_test_data.sh helper:
bash tests/collect_test_data.sh
TEST_LEGACY_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
Rscript tests/run_tests.R
The tests skip automatically when the environment variables are not set, so unit-only runs are unaffected.


3. Render the interactive notebook
The notebook at tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.qmd produces an HTML report with concordance tables, score distribution histograms, heatmaps, and per-QTL interval detail. To render it after a pipeline run:
quarto render tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.qmd \
  -P legacy_assessment:/path/to/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
  -P db_assessment:/path/to/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
  -P db_dir:/path/to/db
Or set environment variables and render without -P flags:
export TEST_LEGACY_ASSESSMENT=/path/to/simulation_assessment_results.tsv
export TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=/path/to/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv
export TEST_DB_DIR=/path/to/db
quarto render tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.qmd
The rendered HTML is written to tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.html.

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
Notebook Contents
The notebook tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.qmd contains:

Data Loading & Overview — reads both assessment TSVs, applies designate_qtl() to classify outcomes, and shows dimension/designation count comparison tables.
Per-Replicate Concordance — computes the three concordance scores for every mapping entry, then presents:

Per-mapping concordance score table
Score distribution histograms
Mean concordance by method and threshold (table + heatmap)
Per-QTL interval Jaccard detail with pass/fail summary

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
Adapting for Other Comparisons
The notebook can be adapted to compare any two assessment TSVs that share the 35-column schema. To compare outputs from different pipeline versions or parameter configurations:

Copy the notebook to a working directory.
Set legacy_assessment_path and db_assessment_path to point to the two TSVs being compared (the variable names reflect the original DB-vs-legacy use case but accept any conforming TSV).
Render with Quarto as described above.

The normalize_algorithm_id() helper handles the algorithm_id format difference between DB-path (LMM-EXACT-INBRED_noPCA_EIGEN) and legacy-path (inbred_nopca_EIGEN) by uppercasing and stripping the LMM-EXACT- prefix. If both TSVs use the same format, this normalization is a no-op.

title: Database Structure
text:
The DB path stores pipeline outputs in a Parquet database queried at analysis time via DuckDB. The database contains five data domains:

Marker sets — static variant data from LD-pruned PLINK files, one file per marker set (identified by marker_set_id)
Genotype matrices — long-format genotype data (CHROM, POS, strain, allele) located in markers/genotypes/, one file per marker set
Mappings — per-marker association statistics from GCTA GWAS output, Hive-partitioned by population and mapping ID
Trait data — per-trait metadata, causal variant parameters, and phenotype values for future offline variance-explained estimation
Metadata tables — lightweight summary lookups for marker sets and mappings without scanning the full data files

{outputDir}/db/
 ├── markers/
 │   ├── marker_sets/
 │   │   └── {marker_set_id}_markers.parquet     ◄── marker data
 │   └── genotypes/
 │       └── {marker_set_id}_genotypes.parquet   ◄── genotype matrix, long format
 ├── mappings/
 │   └── population={pop}/
 │       └── mapping_id={id}/
 │           └── data.parquet                    ◄── GWA statistics
 ├── traits/
 │   ├── {trait_id}.parquet                      ◄── trait metadata
 │   ├── causal_variants/
 │   │   └── {trait_id}_causal.parquet           ◄── causal variant parameters
 │   └── phenotypes/
 │       └── {trait_id}_phenotype.parquet        ◄── pre-upscaled phenotype values
 ├── mappings_metadata.parquet                   ◄── mapping summary table
 └── marker_set_metadata.parquet                 ◄── EIGEN + marker counts + strainfile provenance
All Parquet files use Snappy compression. Schemas are defined as Arrow schema objects in R/database.R.

title: Database Structure
text:
Marker Sets
A marker set represents the LD-pruned variant panel for a given population, MAF threshold, species, VCF release, and LD pruning threshold. Marker sets are written once during database ingestion and queried repeatedly during QTL analysis. The marker set identity is encoded in the marker_set_id v=2 hash.

Data Source
Marker data originates from two upstream processes:







Source
Columns provided




PLINK .bim file (PLINK_RECODE_MS_VCF)
marker, CHROM, POS, A1, A2


EIGEN independent tests file (LOCAL_COMPILE_EIGENS)
n_independent_tests (metadata only)



Source: R/io.R::read_bim_file()


Markers Schema
File: {base_dir}/markers/marker_sets/{marker_set_id}_markers.parquet








Column
Arrow Type
Description




marker_set_id
utf8
20-char SHA-256 hash identifying the marker set


marker
utf8
Variant identifier in CHROM:POS format (e.g. 1:157580)


CHROM
utf8
Chromosome (numeric string: "1", "2", …, "6")


POS
int32
Genomic position (bp)


A1
utf8
Allele 1 (minor allele from PLINK)


A2
utf8
Allele 2 (major allele from PLINK)



Key: marker_set_id uniquely identifies a marker set. Within a marker set, marker (equivalently CHROM + POS) is unique.
Source: R/database.R::markers_schema(), R/database.R::write_marker_set()


Marker Set Metadata Schema
File: {base_dir}/marker_set_metadata.parquet








Column
Arrow Type
Description




marker_set_id
utf8
20-char SHA-256 hash (primary key)


marker_set_hash_string
utf8
Human-readable hash input string


hash_schema_version
utf8
"v=2" — encodes population, maf, species, vcf_release_id, ms_ld


population
utf8
Population/strain set identifier


maf
float64
MAF threshold for marker SNP selection


species
utf8
Species identifier (c_elegans, c_briggsae, etc.)


vcf_release_id
utf8
8-digit CaeNDR release date or VCF filename date


ms_ld
float64
LD R² threshold used for marker SNP LD pruning


n_markers
int32
Total number of markers


n_independent_tests
float64
Independent tests from EIGEN decomposition


eigen_source_file
utf8
EIGEN source filename


strainfile_hash
utf8
SHA-256 hex of the complete strainfile at submission time


strain_list
utf8
Comma-separated strain names used for this population group


created_at
timestamp[us]
Write timestamp



Key: marker_set_id uniquely identifies a record.
This table is used by get_threshold_params() to retrieve marker counts and EIGEN values for significance threshold calculation without reading the full marker Parquet files.
Source: R/database.R::marker_set_metadata_schema(), R/database.R::write_marker_set_metadata()


Example: Querying a Marker Set
# Via R helper function
markers <- read_marker_set("complete", 0.05, base_dir = "results/db")

# Via DuckDB (used internally by open_mapping_db)
ms_meta <- read_marker_set_metadata("complete", 0.05, base_dir = "results/db")
ms_id <- generate_marker_set_id(
  "complete", 0.05,
  ms_meta$species, ms_meta$vcf_release_id, as.numeric(ms_meta$ms_ld)
)$hash
con <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), ":memory:")
DBI::dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE VIEW markers AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('results/db/markers/marker_sets/*_markers.parquet')
")
result <- DBI::dbGetQuery(con, glue::glue("  # glue interpolates {ms_id}
  SELECT COUNT(*) as n_markers, MIN(POS) as min_pos, MAX(POS) as max_pos
  FROM markers
  WHERE marker_set_id = '{ms_id}'
  GROUP BY CHROM
  ORDER BY CHROM
"))

title: Database Structure
text:
Genotype Matrices
A genotype matrix stores the LD-pruned variant calls for a given population and MAF threshold. Genotype matrices are written once during database ingestion and are available for future offline variance-explained estimation.
Genotype files are located in markers/genotypes/ alongside the marker Parquet files in markers/marker_sets/. The markers DuckDB view uses a narrowed glob (_markers.parquet$) to exclude genotype files from marker queries.

Data Source
Genotype data originates from the wide-format TSV produced by BCFTOOLS_CREATE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX. The TSV has one column per strain plus CHROM, POS, REF, and ALT columns. The write function:

Drops REF and ALT (redundant with the markers file)
Pivots from wide format to long format via data.table::melt()
Applies strict biallelic encoding: −1 (hom ref), 1 (hom alt), NA (heterozygous or missing)

Source: R/database.R::write_genotype_matrix()


Genotype Matrix Schema
File: {base_dir}/markers/genotypes/{marker_set_id}_genotypes.parquet








Column
Arrow Type
Description




marker_set_id
utf8
20-char SHA-256 hash (join key to markers)


CHROM
utf8
Chromosome identifier


POS
int32
Genomic position (bp)


strain
utf8
Strain name


allele
float64
−1.0 (hom ref), 1.0 (hom alt), NA (het/missing)



Key: (marker_set_id, CHROM, POS, strain) uniquely identifies a row.
This fixed 5-column schema avoids dynamic Arrow schemas when strain panels vary across populations. At production scale (7,000 markers × 200 strains) the long-format file contains ~1.4 million rows; CHROM and strain have low cardinality and compress well with Parquet dictionary encoding.
Source: R/database.R::genotype_matrix_schema(), R/database.R::write_genotype_matrix(), R/database.R::read_genotype_matrix()

title: Database Structure
text:
Mappings
A mapping stores the per-marker association statistics from a single GWAS run. Each mapping corresponds to one combination of simulation parameters and mapping method. Mappings are written during database ingestion and queried during QTL analysis and assessment.

Data Source
Mapping data originates from GCTA GWA output files:








GWA Mode
File Format
Columns




inbred (fastGWA-mlm-exact)
.fastGWA
CHR, SNP, POS, A1, A2, N, AF1, BETA, SE, P


loco (mlma-loco)
.mlma
Chr, SNP, bp, A1, A2, Freq, b, se, p



Both formats are normalized to a common schema by read_raw_gwa_file(). Key normalizations:

Column names standardized (e.g. Chr → CHROM, bp → POS, Freq → AF1)
marker column created from SNP column (already in CHROM:POS format)
N column dropped (absent from .mlma, unused downstream)
log10p not stored — computed at query time via safe_log10p(P) in R/analysis.R

LOCO deduplication: .mlma files from LOCO mapping may contain duplicate marker rows. These are deduplicated by distinct(marker, .keep_all = TRUE) before writing.
Source: R/io.R::read_raw_gwa_file()


Mappings Schema
File: {base_dir}/mappings/population={pop}/mapping_id={id}/data.parquet



Column
Arrow Type
Description




marker
utf8
Variant identifier in CHROM:POS format


AF1
float64
Allele frequency from GWA output


BETA
float64
Effect size estimate


SE
float64
Standard error of effect estimate


P
float64
P-value from association test









Note



mapping_id is the Hive partition key (encoded in the directory path mappings/population={pop}/mapping_id={id}/) and is auto-promoted by DuckDB when the directory tree is scanned — it does not appear as a column inside the Parquet file. marker_set_id and trait_id are stored in mappings_metadata.parquet (accessed via the metadata DuckDB view) and are not repeated in the data files. Simulation parameters (nqtl, rep, h2, effect, algorithm, pca, population, maf) are recoverable via mappings_metadata without duplicating them across millions of data rows.


Source: R/database.R::mappings_schema(), R/database.R::write_mapping_partitioned()


Mapping ID Format
Each ID in the three-level hierarchy is a deterministic 20-character SHA-256 prefix computed from a canonical input string:
marker_set_id  = SHA-256("v=2|population={pop}|maf={maf:.10f}|species={species}|vcf_release_id={vcf_id}|ms_ld={ms_ld:.10f}")[1:20]
  └── trait_id = SHA-256("v=1|parent={marker_set_hash}|nqtl={n}|effect={e}|rep={r}|h2={h2:.10f}")[1:20]
        └── mapping_id = SHA-256("v=1|parent={trait_hash}|algorithm={algorithm}|pca={TRUE|FALSE}")[1:20]
Golden values (pinned 2026-03-03, v=2): ("ce.test" population, c_elegans, release 20220216, ms_ld 0.8 — values verified against R/database.R in S06.)
generate_marker_set_id("ce.test", 0.05, "c_elegans", "20220216", 0.8)$hash
# → "db39e634ef0bfa76e1bd"

generate_trait_id("db39e634ef0bfa76e1bd", 5, "gamma", 1, 0.8)$hash
# → "aadfdfe371a917896ad6"

generate_mapping_id("aadfdfe371a917896ad6", "inbred", TRUE)$hash
# → "c7e06e757cd1102e6092"
Source: R/database.R::generate_marker_set_id(), R/database.R::generate_trait_id(), R/database.R::generate_mapping_id()


Algorithm Values
The algorithm column encodes the GCTA mapping method:



Nextflow mode
Database algorithm
GCTA Method




inbred
inbred
--fastGWA-mlm-exact


loco
loco
--mlma-loco



The pca boolean tracks the Nextflow type parameter:



Nextflow type
Database pca




pca
TRUE


nopca
FALSE





Hive Partitioning
Mappings use Hive-style directory partitioning:
mappings/
 └── population=complete/
     ├── mapping_id=5b9eecbb85032068d7e4/   # inbred + PCA
     │   └── data.parquet
     └── mapping_id=a3f7d2e1c9b04f8e2d1a/   # inbred + noPCA
         └── data.parquet
This structure enables:

True parallel writes — each Nextflow process writes to a unique directory with no file-level coordination
Partition pruning — DuckDB reads only the partitions matching a WHERE clause filter
Incremental population — new mappings can be added without rewriting existing files

DuckDB reads the partitioned structure with:
CREATE VIEW mappings AS
SELECT * FROM read_parquet(
  '{base_dir}/mappings/**/*.parquet',
  hive_partitioning = true,
  union_by_name = true
)
Source: R/database.R::write_mapping_partitioned(), R/queries.R::open_mapping_db()

title: Database Structure
text:
Mappings Metadata
The mappings metadata table provides a lightweight summary of all mappings in the database. It is built by DB_MIGRATION_AGGREGATE_METADATA after all GWA writes complete, enabling fast lookups without scanning the full Parquet files.

Mappings Metadata Schema
File: {base_dir}/mappings_metadata.parquet








Column
Arrow Type
Description




mapping_id
utf8
20-char SHA-256 hash (primary key)


mapping_hash_string
utf8
Human-readable hash input string (metadata only)


trait_id
utf8
FK → trait metadata (direct parent)


marker_set_id
utf8
FK → marker set metadata (grandparent; for query convenience)


hash_schema_version
utf8
"v=1"


population
utf8
Population/strain set identifier


maf
float64
MAF threshold


nqtl
int32
Number of simulated causal QTLs


rep
int32
Simulation replicate number


h2
float64
Simulated heritability


effect
utf8
Effect size distribution


algorithm
utf8
Mapping algorithm ("inbred" or "loco")


pca
boolean
Whether PCA covariate was included


n_markers
int32
Number of markers in this mapping


source_file
utf8
Original partition directory name


processed_at
timestamp[us]
When metadata was aggregated


processing_version
utf8
Pipeline version string



Key: mapping_id uniquely identifies each record.
Source: R/database.R::metadata_schema(), modules/db_migration/aggregate_metadata/resources/usr/bin/aggregate_metadata.R

title: Database Structure
text:
Trait Data
Phase 5 added three per-trait data stores to the database. Together with the genotype matrix, these tables contain everything needed to estimate variance-explained offline without re-running the pipeline.
Trait data is written by DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_TRAIT_DATA from GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES output (pre-mode-crossing, so each trait is written exactly once). These writes are fire-and-forget storage operations with no downstream dependents within the pipeline.

Trait IDs
Traits are identified by a deterministic 20-character SHA-256 hash derived from the parent marker set hash and simulation parameters:
trait_id = SHA-256("v=1|parent={marker_set_hash}|nqtl={n}|effect={e}|rep={r}|h2={h2:.10f}")[1:20]
The caller must first compute the marker set ID and pass its hash as the parent. No Groovy counterpart exists — the Nextflow .multiMap block passes raw parameters to the R script, which computes trait_id internally. This eliminates cross-language hash divergence.
Properties:

Resume-safe: identical parameters → identical trait_id across pipeline -resume → Nextflow caches the write processes
Debuggable: trait_id can be reconstructed from parameters alone, without scanning the database
Unique: any script can call generate_trait_id(ms_hash, ...) to locate stored data without a directory scan

Source: R/database.R::generate_trait_id()
ms_id    <- generate_marker_set_id("complete", 0.05, "c_elegans", "20220216", 0.8)
trait    <- generate_trait_id(ms_id$hash, nqtl = 5, effect = "gamma", rep = 1, h2 = 0.8)
trait_id <- trait$hash   # 20-char hex string


Trait Metadata Schema
File: {base_dir}/traits/{trait_id}.parquet








Column
Arrow Type
Description




trait_id
utf8
20-char SHA-256 hash (primary key)


trait_hash_string
utf8
Human-readable hash input string (metadata only)


marker_set_id
utf8
FK → marker set metadata (parent link)


nqtl
int32
Number of simulated causal QTLs


rep
int32
Simulation replicate number


sim_seed
int32
Integer seed used for RNG initialization in causal variant and effect size simulation; equals rep for runs using the default seed strategy


h2
float64
Simulated heritability


maf
float64
MAF threshold


effect
utf8
Effect size distribution (e.g. "gamma")


population
utf8
Population/strain group identifier


created_at
utf8
ISO 8601 write timestamp



Key: trait_id uniquely identifies each record. Re-running with the same parameters writes to the same path; overwrite = TRUE replaces the file cleanly.
Source: R/database.R::trait_metadata_schema(), R/database.R::write_trait_metadata(), R/database.R::read_trait_metadata()


Causal Variants Schema
File: {base_dir}/traits/causal_variants/{trait_id}_causal.parquet



Column
Arrow Type
Description




QTL
utf8
QTL label from GCTA .par file


CHROM
utf8
Chromosome


POS
int32
Genomic position (bp)


RefAllele
utf8
Reference allele


Frequency
float64
Allele frequency of the causal variant


Effect
float64
Simulated effect size



Causal variant records are loaded from the GCTA .par file via assessment.R::load_causal_variants().
Source: R/database.R::write_causal_variants(), R/database.R::read_causal_variants_data()


Phenotype Schema
File: {base_dir}/traits/phenotypes/{trait_id}_phenotype.parquet



Column
Arrow Type
Description




strain
utf8
Strain name (FID column from .phen file)


phenotype
float64
Simulated phenotype value









Important



Pre-upscaled values. The stored phenotype is the direct output of GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES, captured before PLINK_UPDATE_BY_H2 → PYTHON_CHECK_VP. The check_vp.py script may multiply values by 1000× when Vp < 1e-6, so phenotype values stored here may differ from the values used in the actual GWAS mapping. For future variance-explained estimation this is immaterial: the ANOVA SS ratio (SSbetween / SStotal) is scale-invariant.


Source: R/database.R::phenotype_schema(), R/database.R::write_phenotype_data(), R/database.R::read_phenotype_data()


Offline Variance-Explained Estimation
With all four data stores populated, variance explained can be estimated for any trait without re-running the pipeline:
ms_id    <- generate_marker_set_id(group, maf, species, vcf_release_id, ms_ld)
trait_id <- generate_trait_id(ms_id$hash, nqtl, effect, rep, h2)$hash

geno   <- read_genotype_matrix(group, maf, species, vcf_release_id, ms_ld, base_dir)  # CHROM, POS, strain, allele
pheno  <- read_phenotype_data(trait_id, base_dir)            # strain, phenotype
causal <- read_causal_variants_data(trait_id, base_dir)      # QTL, CHROM, POS, Effect, ...

# ANOVA SS method (matching legacy Assess_Sims.R):
#   var_exp = SS_between / SS_total per marker
# Join geno + pheno on strain, filter to causal positions, compute SS ratio.

title: Database Structure
text:
Query Patterns
The database is accessed through DuckDB via helper functions in R/queries.R. All queries are read-only; writes go through R/database.R.

Connection Setup
open_mapping_db() creates an in-memory DuckDB connection and registers four views:








View
Source
Description




markers
markers/marker_sets/*_markers.parquet
All marker set files (excludes genotype files)


mappings
mappings/**/*.parquet (Hive)
All mapping partitions


metadata
mappings_metadata.parquet
Mapping summary table


marker_set_metadata
marker_set_metadata.parquet
EIGEN values and marker counts





Key Query Functions








Function
Source
Purpose




query_for_threshold_analysis()
R/queries.R
Retrieve mapping data with marker coordinates joined — primary query for QTL detection


get_threshold_params()
R/database.R
Look up marker count and EIGEN independent tests for threshold calculation


query_by_mapping_id()
R/queries.R
Retrieve raw mapping data for a single mapping


query_mapping_data()
R/queries.R
Full mapping + marker join with optional filters


get_metadata()
R/queries.R
Read the mappings metadata summary table


list_populations()
R/queries.R
List available populations from partition directories


db_stats()
R/queries.R
Database-wide statistics (file counts, sizes, populations)

The query_for_threshold_analysis() function is the primary entry point used by analyze_qtl.R and assess_sims.R. It joins mappings with markers to resolve chromosome coordinates and allele frequencies, and validates CHROM:POS uniqueness per mapping.


Connection Caching
For batch operations, get_db_connection() provides a cached DuckDB connection that persists across multiple queries. The cache is keyed by base_dir and automatically reconnects if the connection becomes stale.
# Single query (creates and closes connection)
data <- query_for_threshold_analysis(mapping_id, base_dir)

# Batch operation (reuses cached connection)
con <- get_db_connection(base_dir, use_cache = TRUE)
for (id in mapping_ids) {
  data <- query_for_threshold_analysis(id, base_dir, con = con)
  # ... process data
}
close_cached_connection(base_dir)

title: nemascan-sims-nf
text: Pipeline documentation for nemascan-sims-nf — a Nextflow pipeline that simulates GWAS for C. elegans and related nematodes, evaluating the power and precision of different mapping algorithms.

title: nemascan-sims-nf
text:
Reports

Marker Set Generation — Phase 1 process documentation covering VCF subsetting, chromosome renaming, LD pruning, and genotype matrix creation.
Trait Simulation — Phase 2 process documentation covering causal variant selection, GCTA phenotype simulation, and PLINK fileset preparation.
GWAS Mapping — Phase 3 process documentation covering GRM construction, phenotypic variance checking, and association mapping under four mode/type conditions.
QTL Detection & Assessment — Phase 4 process documentation covering database ingestion, QTL interval detection, and performance assessment through both the DB and legacy paths.
Database Structure — Parquet database schema reference for marker sets, genotype matrices, simulated trait mappings, trait data (metadata, causal variants, phenotypes), and metadata tables.
Assessment Cross-Validation — How to run and interpret the cross-validation analysis comparing DB-path and legacy-path assessment outputs.
Development — Contributing guide covering documentation authoring, GitHub Pages publishing, testing, and project structure.

title: Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment
text:
The final phase of the pipeline takes GWA results from Phase 3, detects QTL intervals, and assesses detection performance against the simulated causal variants. Two parallel paths produce equivalent output:

DB path (default) — writes results to a Parquet database and queries via DuckDB
Legacy path (opt-in via --legacy_assess) — operates directly on intermediate files using R and GenomicRanges

GWA results (from Phase 3)
 │
 ├─── DB PATH (default) ──────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                                                                │
 │  Stage 1: Database Ingestion                                   │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_MARKER_SET  (barrier)                    │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX                          │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GWA_TO_DB                                │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_TRAIT_DATA                               │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_AGGREGATE_METADATA (barrier)                   │
 │                                                                │
 │  Stage 2: QTL Analysis                                         │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_ANALYZE_QTL ──► *_qtl_regions.tsv              │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_ASSESS_SIMS ──► *_db_assessment.tsv            │
 │              ▼                                                 │
 │    db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv                         │
 │                                                                │
 ├─── LEGACY PATH (--legacy_assess) ──────────────────────────────┐
 │                                                                │
 │    R_GET_GCTA_INTERVALS ──► processed mapping + QTL regions    │
 │    R_ASSESS_SIMS ──────────► *_mapping.tsv                     │
 │              ▼                                                 │
 │    simulation_assessment_results.tsv                            │
 └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Both paths expand each upstream GWA result by threshold — every (mode, type) combination is analyzed once for BF and once for EIGEN — doubling the output relative to Phase 3.
For a comparison of the two paths, see the Assessment Cross-Validation documentation.

title: Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment
text:
Shared Concepts
Both paths implement the same logical algorithm for QTL interval detection. The key concepts are described here once and referenced in the path-specific sections below.

Significance Thresholds
A significance threshold converts raw p-values into a binary significant/not significant call for each marker. The pipeline supports three methods:








Method
Formula
Source




BF (Bonferroni)
-log10(alpha / n_markers)
Total marker count from the marker set


EIGEN
-log10(alpha / n_independent_tests)
Eigenvalue decomposition of the genotype matrix


User-defined
Numeric value used directly
Passed via --sthresh parameter



Where alpha defaults to 0.05. The threshold is applied on the -log10(p) scale.
Source: R/analysis.R::calculate_threshold()


QTL Interval Detection Algorithm
Both paths apply the same four-step procedure to flag significant markers, group them into QTL intervals, and define confidence intervals:

Flag significant markers — compute -log10(p) for each marker and flag those where log10p >= threshold as significant (significant = 1 in DB path, aboveBF = 1 in legacy path)
Proportion check — if more than 15% of markers are significant, the threshold is considered too lenient and QTL intervals are not defined (all interval columns set to NA)
Group significant markers by proximity — significant markers are assigned sequential peak_id values. Two significant markers on the same chromosome within group_qtl marker-index distance are assigned to the same peak; otherwise a new peak ID is assigned
Define confidence intervals — for each peak group, the CI boundary is set to ci_size markers beyond the outermost significant marker in the group, clamped to chromosome bounds. The peak marker is the one with the highest -log10(p) in the group

Parameters:








Parameter
Default
Description




--group_qtl
1000
Marker-index distance for grouping significant markers into the same QTL


--ci_size
150
Number of markers flanking the peak group to define the CI boundary


--alpha
0.05
Significance level for threshold calculation



Source (DB path): R/analysis.R::analyze_mapping() which calls flag_significant_markers() → check_significant_proportion() → group_markers_to_intervals() → define_confidence_intervals()
Source (legacy path): bin/Get_GCTA_Intervals.R::process_mapping_df() which implements the same logic inline


Assessment Classification
After QTL intervals are defined, each QTL (simulated or detected) is classified into one of four outcome categories:










Designation
Simulated
Detected
Significant
Meaning




Detected.CV
TRUE
TRUE
TRUE
True positive — causal variant within a detected interval, peak is significant


Missed.CV
TRUE
FALSE
FALSE
False negative — causal variant not within any detected interval


CV.Not.Significant.In.Interval
TRUE
TRUE
FALSE
Causal variant within a detected interval, but its own marker is not significant


False.Discovery
FALSE
TRUE
TRUE
False positive — detected interval contains no causal variant



Source: R/sim_performance.R::designate_qtl()

title: Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment

Stage 1: Database Ingestion (DB Path Only)
The DB path writes marker sets and raw GWA results to a Hive-partitioned Parquet database before performing QTL analysis. This enables DuckDB-based queries at analysis time and decouples threshold application from data storage.
Five processes run during Stage 1. DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_MARKER_SET and DB_MIGRATION_AGGREGATE_METADATA act as barriers. DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX and DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_TRAIT_DATA run in parallel as independent fire-and-forget storage operations — they have no downstream dependents within the pipeline:

The stored phenotype is pre-upscaled (GCTA output before PYTHON_CHECK_VP). Values may differ from those used in the actual GWAS mapping by up to 1000× if check_vp.py applied upscaling. This is intentional — the ANOVA SS ratio for offline variance-explained estimation is scale-invariant.




5. DB_MIGRATION_AGGREGATE_METADATA
Source: modules/db_migration/aggregate_metadata/main.nf  •  Script: resources/usr/bin/aggregate_metadata.R
Scans all mapping Parquet files and builds a metadata summary table. Runs once after all DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GWA_TO_DB processes complete.
Command:

The script reads the first row of each Parquet file to extract FK hash columns (mapping_id, marker_set_id, trait_id) and counts total markers per file.
Barrier role: The database must be fully populated before analysis queries begin. The summary output gates all downstream DB_MIGRATION_ANALYZE_QTL invocations.

Direction
File
Description




Input

All mapping Parquet files


Output

Output

Human-readable summary (barrier signal)





Database Structure
The Parquet database uses a Hive partitioning scheme:
{outputDir}/db/
 ├── markers/
 │   ├── marker_sets/
 │   │   └── {marker_set_id}_markers.parquet
 │   └── genotypes/
 │       └── {marker_set_id}_genotypes.parquet
 ├── mappings/
 │   └── population={pop}/
 │       └── mapping_id={id}/
 │           └── data.parquet
 ├── traits/
 │   ├── {trait_id}.parquet                      ◄── trait metadata
 │   ├── causal_variants/
 │   │   └── {trait_id}_causal.parquet           ◄── causal variant parameters
 │   └── phenotypes/
 │       └── {trait_id}_phenotype.parquet        ◄── pre-upscaled phenotype values
 ├── mappings_metadata.parquet
 └── marker_set_metadata.parquet
At query time, DuckDB reads the partitioned Parquet files directly via the read_parquet() function with hive_partitioning = true. This allows efficient filtering by population and mapping ID without loading all data into memory. See R/queries.R::open_mapping_db() for the connection setup.
The traits/ directory and its causal_variants/ and phenotypes/ subdirectories are written by DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_TRAIT_DATA and are not read by any pipeline process — they are stored for future offline analysis. See Database Structure for full schemas.

title: Phase 3: GWAS Mapping
text:
The third phase of the pipeline maps simulated phenotypes using GCTA to evaluate GWA power and precision. It takes the PLINK filesets from Phase 2 and produces per-marker association statistics under four mapping conditions.
The processing flow is:
PLINK binaries (from Phase 2)
 │
 ├─ GCTA_MAKE_GRM ──────────► GRM binaries + check_vp.hsq
 │                                   │
 ▼                                   ▼
 PYTHON_CHECK_VP ────────────► variance-checked .pheno file
 │
 ▼
 GCTA_PERFORM_GWA ───────────► .fastGWA or .mlma association results
                                 │
                                 ├─ Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment
                                 └─ DB Migration: write_gwa_to_db.R
Each process is described below with the exact commands run, key parameters, and links to external tool documentation.
Mode and type parameterization: The mapping phase is parameterized by two variables defined in main.nf:

mode: "inbred" or "loco" — determines the type of GRM and the GWA algorithm. These lowercase values are the canonical algorithm identifiers used in database mapping IDs.
type: "pca" or "nopca" — determines whether the first principal component eigenvector is included as a covariate. type == "pca" maps to pca = TRUE in the database mapping ID.

ch_mode = Channel.of(
    ["inbred", "fastGWA"],
    ["loco", "mlma"]
)

ch_type = Channel.of(
    "pca",
    "nopca"
)
Each simulation replicate is mapped under all four combinations (inbred x pca, inbred x nopca, loco x pca, loco x nopca), driven by Nextflow channel combine operations. The mode crossing happens at GCTA_MAKE_GRM and the type crossing happens at GCTA_PERFORM_GWA.

title: Phase 3: GWAS Mapping
text:
1. GCTA_MAKE_GRM
Source: modules/gcta/make_grm/main.nf
Builds a genetic relatedness matrix (GRM) and estimates phenotypic variance via REML. This process runs two sequential GCTA commands.

Step 1: Build the GRM
The GRM construction method depends on the mode parameter:
if [[ ${mode} == "inbred" ]]; then
    GRM_OPTION="--make-grm-inbred"
else
    GRM_OPTION="--make-grm"
fi

gcta64 --bfile TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group} \
        --autosome --maf ${maf} ${GRM_OPTION} \
        --out TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --thread-num 1



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--make-grm-inbred
Construct a kinship matrix for inbred organisms, adjusting for reduced heterozygosity
GCTA GRM


--make-grm
Construct a standard genome-wide relatedness matrix
GCTA GRM


--autosome
Use only autosomal markers
GCTA GRM


--maf
Filter markers below the minor allele frequency threshold
GCTA GRM




mode == "inbred": Uses --make-grm-inbred, which constructs a kinship matrix tailored for populations of inbred organisms. The inbred GRM adjusts relatedness estimates to account for the reduced heterozygosity characteristic of inbred lines.
mode == "loco": Uses --make-grm, which constructs a standard genome-wide relatedness matrix using all autosomal markers.

Because main.nf defines ch_mode with both ["inbred", "fastGWA"] and ["loco", "mlma"], each simulation replicate produces two GRMs — one inbred and one standard.


Step 2: REML variance estimation
gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --pheno ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.phen \
        --reml --out check_vp \
        --thread-num 1
This estimates the phenotypic variance (Vp) from the simulated phenotype data using restricted maximum likelihood (REML). See GCTA GREML analysis documentation.



Flag
Purpose




--grm
Input GRM prefix from step 1


--pheno
Simulated phenotype file


--reml
Perform REML analysis to estimate variance components

df  1

n   96
The key value is Vp (total phenotypic variance), which is checked in the next process.

title: Phase 3: GWAS Mapping
text:
2. PYTHON_CHECK_VP
Source: modules/python/check_vp.nf
Inspects the estimated phenotypic variance (Vp) from the check_vp.hsq file produced by GCTA REML. This step ensures that simulated phenotypes exhibit sufficient variance, preventing near-zero variance from causing numerical issues in the association mapping step.
Command:
python3 check_vp.py --check_vp ${hsq_in} --simulated_phenos tmp.pheno
mv new_phenos.temp ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.pheno

Script: bin/check_vp.py







Function
Description




get_vp()
Reads the check_vp.hsq file and extracts the Vp value from the row where Source == “Vp”


increase_pheno_var()
Multiplies all phenotype values (column 3) by 1000


read_original_phenos()
Reads the phenotype file unchanged


write_phenos_to_temp()
Writes output to new_phenos.temp





Variance check logic

Parse check_vp.hsq to extract the Vp value
If Vp < 0.000001: multiply all phenotype values by 1000 to increase variance
If Vp >= 0.000001: retain original phenotype values without modification
Write output to new_phenos.temp, then rename to the final *_sims.pheno filename

If the .hsq file is missing or Vp cannot be parsed, the script falls back to using the original phenotype values.


Example .pheno output
From test data (data/test_data/.../PYTHON_CHECK_VP/5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_ce.96.allout15_irrepressible.grosbeak_sims.pheno):
MY2713 MY2713 -6.04369
JU323 JU323 11.1615
XZ1734 XZ1734 4.15316
QG4080 QG4080 9.589
In this case Vp was well above the threshold, so phenotype values are unchanged from the GCTA simulation output.

title: Phase 3: GWAS Mapping
text:
3. GCTA_PERFORM_GWA
Source: modules/gcta/perform_gwa/main.nf
Performs genome-wide association mapping. This process receives the GRM and variance-checked phenotype data, then executes three sub-steps controlled by the mode and type parameters.

Step 1: Create sparse GRM
This step always runs regardless of mode or type:
gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
    --make-bK-sparse ${sparse_cut} \
    --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
    --thread-num 1



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--make-bK-sparse
Set all off-diagonal GRM values with absolute value ≤ sparse_cut (default: 0.05) to zero
GCTA GRM



This produces a sparse representation of the GRM, reducing memory usage for the association mapping step.


Step 2: Conditional PCA
The type parameter controls whether PCA eigenvectors are extracted as covariates:
if [[ ${type} == "pca" ]]; then
    gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --pca 1 \
        --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
        --thread-num 1

    COVAR="--qcovar ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode}.eigenvec"
else
    COVAR=""
fi

type == "pca": Extracts the first principal component from the GRM (--pca 1), producing an .eigenvec file. This eigenvector is passed as a quantitative covariate (--qcovar) to the mapping command to control for population structure.
type == "nopca": No PCA is performed. The COVAR variable is set to an empty string, so no covariate is included in the mapping command.



Step 3: Association mapping
The mode parameter determines both the GCTA mapping algorithm and the GRM input flag:
if [[ ${mode} == "inbred" ]]; then
    GRM_OPTION='--grm-sparse'
    COMMAND='--fastGWA-mlm-exact'
else
    GRM_OPTION="--grm"
    COMMAND="--mlma-loco"
fi

GWA_THREADS=$( [[ ${mode} == "loco" ]] && echo ${task.cpus} || echo 1 )

gcta64 ${COMMAND} \
    --bfile TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group} \
    ${GRM_OPTION} ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
    ${COVAR} \
    --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type} \
    --pheno ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.pheno \
    --maf ${maf} \
    --thread-num ${GWA_THREADS}

Thread count: --fastGWA-mlm-exact (inbred mode) uses --thread-num 1 for deterministic results. --mlma-loco (loco mode) uses --thread-num ${task.cpus}, set via the GWA_THREADS shell variable, because its per-chromosome refits are independent and numerically less sensitive. See Thread-count determinism.

Inbred mode (mode == "inbred"):

Uses --fastGWA-mlm-exact to perform an exact mixed linear model (MLM) association analysis (GCTA fastGWA docs)
Uses --grm-sparse to input the sparse GRM created from the inbred relatedness matrix
Output format: .fastGWA (columns: CHR, SNP, POS, A1, A2, N, AF1, BETA, SE, P)

LOCO mode (mode == "loco"):

Uses --mlma-loco to perform MLM-based association using a leave-one-chromosome-out approach, where the GRM excludes the chromosome of the tested variant (GCTA MLMA docs)
Uses --grm to input the sparse GRM created from the standard relatedness matrix
Output format: .loco.mlma (columns: Chr, SNP, bp, A1, A2, Freq, b, se, p), renamed to .mlma after completion

if [[ ${mode} == "loco" ]]; then
    mv "${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type}.loco.mlma" \
       "${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type}.mlma"
fi


Summary of the four mapping conditions

Mapping identifiers
Each of the four mapping conditions is assigned a unique mapping_id by write_gwa_to_db.R during database migration. The ID is a 20-character SHA-256 hash computed from the parent trait hash and the two condition parameters:
mapping_id = SHA-256("v=1|parent={trait_hash}|algorithm={algorithm}|pca={pca_str}")[0:20]
where:

{trait_hash} is the 20-char parent trait_id hash (itself derived from marker_set_id)
{algorithm} is the lowercased mode value: "inbred" or "loco"
{pca_str} is "TRUE" if type == "pca", otherwise "FALSE"

The full hash hierarchy is:
marker_set_id  (population + maf)
  └── trait_id (marker_set_id + nqtl + effect + rep + h2)
        └── mapping_id (trait_id + algorithm + pca)
Because each level embeds its parent hash, a mapping_id uniquely encodes the entire simulation and mapping parameter chain. generate_mapping_id() normalizes algorithm to lowercase via tolower(). The canonical algorithm values are "inbred" and "loco", matching val(mode) from this process after normalization.
When mapping results are written to the database, only the measurement columns are stored in each data.parquet file:



Column
Description




marker
Marker ID (CHROM:POS format)


AF1
Effect allele frequency


BETA
Effect size estimate


SE
Standard error


P
P-value



mapping_id is the Hive partition key encoded in the directory path (mappings/population={pop}/mapping_id={id}/) and is auto-promoted by DuckDB when the directory tree is scanned — it is not a column inside the Parquet file. marker_set_id and trait_id are in mappings_metadata.parquet (accessible via the metadata DuckDB view). Simulation parameters (nqtl, rep, h2, effect, algorithm, pca, population, maf) are stored once in mappings_metadata.parquet and recovered via joins — they are not repeated in every data row.

All GCTA steps except --mlma-loco are pinned to --thread-num 1 to ensure reproducible floating-point results; see Thread-count determinism in the developer notes for the full rationale.

title: Phase 2: Trait Simulation
text:
The second phase of the pipeline transforms the LD-pruned PLINK binary filesets (from Phase 1) into simulated phenotype data with known causal variants. These outputs feed into GWAS mapping (Phase 3).
The processing flow is:
MS PLINK binaries (PLINK_RECODE_MS_VCF, Phase 1)
 └─► [Phase 3: GWAS Mapping — unchanged]

CV PLINK binaries (PLINK_RECODE_CV_VCF, Phase 1)
 └─► PYTHON_SIMULATE_EFFECTS_GLOBAL ──► causal_variants.txt
       │ (cv_plink output: CV_TO_SIMS.*)
       ▼
      GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES ──────► .phen + .par files
       │ (MS plink pass-through: TO_SIMS.*)
       ▼
      PLINK_UPDATE_BY_H2 ───────────► Phenotype-annotated MS PLINK binaries
                                       (to Phase 3: GWAS Mapping)
Each process is described below with the exact commands run, key parameters, and links to external tool documentation.
Cartesian product expansion: The each qualifiers on rep, nqtl, effect, and h2 in the Nextflow process definitions create all parameter combinations. Every PLINK fileset from Phase 1 is crossed with every combination of replicate number, number of QTL, effect size distribution, and heritability value, so downstream processes run independently for each point in the parameter grid.
